fa: Has just four strings but can produce an enormous range of expressive tones, central to Western classical music
Slightly larger than a violin and tuned a fifth lower, often overlooked despite its warm tone
Played upright between the knees, its range is closest to the human voice
The largest and lowest-pitched instrument in the orchestral string family
One of the most popular instruments in the world, central to rock, pop, blues and folk music
Uses nylon strings and is plucked with fingers rather than a pick
Provides the low-end foundation in most modern rock, pop and jazz ensembles
One of the oldest string instruments, with pedal harps having up to 47 strings

ep: West Africa, adapted in colonial America 17th-18th century
fa: Descended from West African instruments, central to American folk and bluegrass music
Has eight strings in four paired courses, common in bluegrass and Italian folk music
A small four-stringed instrument developed in Hawaii from Portuguese instruments
A long-necked Indian instrument with sympathetic strings that resonate beneath the main ones

ep: Japan, adapted from China around the 7th-8th century
fa: A traditional Japanese zither with 13 strings, played by plucking with picks
A Russian instrument with a distinctive triangular body and three strings
A pear-shaped, fretless Middle Eastern lute considered an ancestor of the guitar
A Chinese two-stringed bowed instrument sometimes called the 'Chinese violin'
Produces sound via a hand-cranked wheel rubbing against the strings like a mechanical violin bow
A small Andean lute traditionally made from an armadillo shell
A percussion-string hybrid where hammers strike strings, invented in Italy around 1700
Plucks its strings rather than striking them, the dominant keyboard instrument before the piano
Pipe organs can have thousands of pipes and are among the largest musical instruments ever built
Uses a hand-pumped bellows to push air through reeds, popular in folk music worldwide
Struck metal bars give it a bell-like sound, famously used in Tchaikovsky's 'Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy'
A quiet Renaissance keyboard instrument that lets players control volume through touch
Generates sound electronically, revolutionizing music production from the 1970s onward

ep: Prehistoric, oldest known examples over 40,000 years old
fa: One of the oldest known instruments, with prehistoric bone flutes found dating back over 40,000 years
A smaller, higher-pitched version of the flute, the highest-pitched instrument in the orchestra
Uses a single reed and has an unusually wide pitch range for a woodwind
Traditionally used to tune the rest of the orchestra before a concert
A double-reed instrument known for its deep, distinctive tone and long, folded body
Invented in the 1840s by Adolphe Sax, made of brass but classified as a woodwind
A simple fipple flute often used to teach music to children in schools

ep: Ancient Middle East, adapted across Europe by the Middle Ages
fa: Uses a constantly filled air bag to sustain continuous sound, iconic in Scottish and Irish music

ep: Ancient Greece and pre-Columbian Andes, independently
fa: Made of multiple tuned pipes of different lengths bound together, found in many ancient cultures

ep: Northern Australia, at least 1,000-1,500 years old
fa: An Indigenous Australian wind instrument that can produce continuous sound through circular breathing
A traditional Japanese bamboo flute historically played by wandering Zen Buddhist monks

ep: Found independently across ancient China, Mesoamerica and Africa
fa: An ancient vessel flute shape found independently in cultures across the globe
One of the oldest brass instruments, with ancient versions found in Egyptian tombs
Uses a sliding tube instead of valves to change pitch, unique among common brass instruments
Its tubing can stretch over 12 feet if uncoiled, giving it a warm, mellow tone
The largest and lowest-pitched instrument in the brass family
Similar to the trumpet but with a more conical bore, giving it a mellower tone
A tenor-voiced brass instrument common in concert and military bands
Has a wider, conical bore than a trumpet, giving it a softer, rounder tone favored in jazz
Designed to be worn wrapped around the body, named after bandleader John Philip Sousa
Assembled in the early 20th century so one musician could play several drums and cymbals at once
Named for the wire snares stretched across its bottom head that give it a sharp, rattling sound

ep: Middle East, adopted in Europe by the 15th century
fa: Large tunable drums that can be adjusted to specific pitches during a performance

ep: Southeast Asia and Africa, independently, over 1,000 years ago
fa: Wooden bars struck with mallets, its name comes from Greek words for 'wood' and 'sound'

ep: Central America and Southern Africa, centuries old
fa: Similar to a xylophone but with resonators beneath the bars for a deeper, richer tone
Uses motor-driven fans in its resonators to create a signature vibrato effect
Metal bars struck with mallets produce a bright, bell-like sound
Combines a drumhead with jingling metal discs called zils around its frame
A box-shaped Peruvian drum played by slapping its front face while sitting on top of it
A goblet-shaped West African hand drum capable of a wide range of tones
A pair of connected Afro-Cuban drums of different sizes played with the hands
A tall, narrow Afro-Cuban drum typically played in sets of two or three
A pair of Indian hand drums central to Hindustani classical music, each tuned differently
Invented in Trinidad and Tobago from repurposed oil drums, now a national instrument
Small hand-held clapper shells traditionally associated with Spanish flamenco dance
A simple metal bar bent into a triangle, capable of surprisingly varied dynamics and tone

ep: Ancient Mesopotamia and Asia Minor, over 3,000 years ago
fa: Metal discs that can be crashed together or struck individually with sticks
A large metal disc used across many Asian cultures, capable of a huge dynamic range
Hand-held rattles filled with seeds or beads, originating in Latin American music
Played without touching it, by moving hands near two antennas that control pitch and volume

ep: Southern Africa, ancient origin, modernized in the 20th century
fa: A modern version of the African mbira, played by plucking metal tines with the thumbs
A traditional Japanese three-stringed instrument played with a large plectrum called a bachi
A long-necked Greek lute central to rebetiko and modern Greek folk music
An Appalachian string instrument traditionally played flat on the lap or a table
Uses button-operated bars to mute unwanted strings, making chords easy to play
A modern Swiss steel instrument shaped like a UFO, played with the hands
Invented by Benjamin Franklin, played by rubbing wet fingers on rotating glass bowls
Played pre-recorded tape loops of real instruments, famously used on The Beatles' 'Strawberry Fields Forever'
Doesn't produce sound itself but modifies the player's own humming through a vibrating membrane
Held between the teeth and plucked, using the mouth as a resonating chamber
A button-operated bellows instrument essential to Argentine tango music

ep: Middle East, adapted in Europe by the 13th century
fa: A pear-shaped plucked instrument that was the most popular instrument in Renaissance Europe
A bowed Renaissance and Baroque instrument played upright between the legs, unlike the modern cello
A flat string instrument played on a table or the lap, central to Central European folk music

ep: France, 1840s, adopted in India in the 19th century
fa: A pump-organ variant central to Indian classical and devotional music
